segments.text: VESICULAR TRAFFIC

MEMBRANE BOUND VESICLES
Proteins do NOT need to cross membranes
Transport between ER, Golgi, lysosomes, exterior

COATED VESICLES
3 main coat types
COPII: from ER to Golgi
COPI: within Golgi and Golgi to ER
Clathrin: between Golgi and cell membrane

VESICULAR MOVEMENT
Budding from donor membrane
Fusion with acceptor/target membrane

COPII COAT FORMATION 1
Regulated by SAR1 (small GTPase protein)
Sar1-GDP: soluble in cytosol
Sar1-GTP: membrane bound
Sec12: Sar1 GEF

COPII COAT FORMATION 2
Sar1-GTP recruits Sec23/24 (coat proteins)
Sec23/24 recruit Sec13/31(more coat proteins)
Coat proteins “curve” membrane
Sec23/24 bind cargo receptors

BUD FORMATION
Continued curvature by coat forms bud
Coat can form cage in vitro (without membrane)

VESICLE “PINCHING OFF”
COPI and COPII vesicles appear to not need “extra” step
For clathrin-coated vesicles membranes brought together by dynamin
Requires GTP hydrolysis

COPII VESICLE UNCOATING
Sar1 hydrolyzes GTP and releases from membrane
Coat disassembles

VESICLE TARGET SPECIFICITY
Vesicle needs to fuse with correct target
Regulated by Rabs and SNAREs

RAB PROTEINS
Specific for different types of membranes
>60 in humans
Rab-GTP: lipid tail bound to membrane
Rab-GDP: lipid tail masked (so cytosolic)

SNAREs
Integral membrane proteins
Dozens of types (each on different membranes)
V-SNARE: SNARE on vesicle
T-SNARE: SNARE on target membrane

VESICLE TARGET RECOGNITION
Rab-GTP binds to Rab effector
V-SNAREs and T-SNAREs bind

MEMBRANE FUSION
SNAREs pull membranes close enough for lipids to move between bilayers

RECYCLING OF SNAREs
Required to return V-SNAREs to donor membrane
NSF and ATP break V-SNARE/T-SNARE binding
GOLGI APPARATUS

GOLGI FUNCTIONS
Sorting of secreted proteins, transmembrane proteins, and lysosomal enzymes
Processing of above proteins
Camillo Golgi

GOLGI ORGANIZATION
Stacks of flat sacs (cisternae)

SEPARATE COMPARTMENTS
Different enzymes within each Golgi compartment

TRANSPORT BETWEEN ER AND GOLGI
COPII-coated vesicles to Golgi
COPI-coated vesicles to ER

LOADING CARGO INTO COPII VESICLE
Soluble cargo binds to membrane receptor
Transmembrane cargo binds to coat directly

SOLUBLE CARGO
Soluble cargo contains FPF signal sequence at N-terminus
FPF binds to Surf4 (a transmembrane receptor)

TRANSMEMBRANE CARGO
Cytosolic domain contains di-acidic signal sequence (DXE)
DXE binds to Sec24 of coat
DXE

RETRIEVAL OF ER PROTEINS
v-SNARES, receptors, ER resident proteins in COPII vesicles
Signal sequences again
Return to ER in COPI-coated vesicles

GOLGI TO ER SIGNAL SEQUENCES
KDEL: at C-terminus of ER soluble proteins
KKXX: at C-terminus of ER transmembrane proteins

REGULATION OF KDEL BINDING
KDEL/receptor binding is pH dependent
Lower pH in Golgi promotes binding

MOVEMENT THROUGH GOLGI
Cisternal maturation model
Secreted protein remains in cisterna (red arrows)
Cisterna “matures” as Golgi enzymes move backward

TRANS-GOLGI NETWORK
Vesicles sorted to trans-Golgi, lysosome, endosome, or cell membrane

EXOCYTOSIS
Signal sequence for transmembrane proteins – YXXF binds to AP1 complex of clathrin coat
Soluble proteins secreted unless sent elsewhere
Constitutive or regulated secretion
LYSOSOMES

LYSOSOME
Function: degradation of macromolecules
Acidic environment
Discovered by Christian de Duve (1974 Nobel Prize)

SOLUBLE LYSOSOMAL ENZYMES
Contain recognition sequence for addition of phosphate to mannose of oligosaccharide
Creates mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) signal
Phosphate added in cis-Golgi

PATHWAY TO LYSOSOME
M6P receptor binds to M6P in TGN
Vesicle fuses with late endosome
Protein separates from receptor (low pH in LE)
Phosphate removed in LE
Transport to lysosome
SOURCES OF MATERIAL FOR DEGRADATION

DEGRADATION OF TRANSMEMBRANE PROTEINS
Problem – digestive enzymes in lumen of lysosome but part of transmembrane protein is cytosolic
Multivesicular bodies form so proteins are within lumen after fusion

FORMATION OF MULTIVESICULAR BODIES
Complex of ESCRT (endosomal sorting complexes required for transport) proteins

FORMATION OF MULTIVESICULAR BODIES (BETTER DETAIL)
See ESCRT video

AUTOPHAGY
Yoshinori Ohsumi – 2016 Nobel Prize
Random mutagenesis of yeast cells
Screen for autophagy defects
Characterized “autophagy-related genes” (Atg) using complementation rescue experiments

MAIN STEPS IN AUTOPHAGY
1 – Phagophore assembly site (PAS) forms
Components to be degraded recruited through selective autophagy receptors (SARs)
2 – PAS recruits Atg9 and COPII vesicles

MAIN STEPS IN AUTOPHAGY
3 – Atg8 needed to add more vesicles
4,5,6 – Autophagosome seals, fuses, and degraded
ENDOCYTOSIS

ENDOCYTOSIS
Uptake of external material in vesicles
Phagocytosis:  ingestion of large particles (e.g., whole bacteria)
Pinocytosis: ingestion of fluids
Receptor-mediated endocytosis: ingestion of specific molecules

RECEPTOR-MEDIATED ENDOCYTOSIS
Membrane receptors bind to specific ligands and enrich these in clathrin-coated vesicles
Examples: LDL and transferrin

LDL
LDL: low density lipoprotein
cholesterol molecules in center
outside
phospholipids
cholesterol
apolipoprotein B
Apolipoprotein B
RME OF LDL/LDL RECEPTOR

LDL RECEPTOR
Binding to ApoB regulated by pH
Cell surface (pH 7) –  ligand-binding arm binds ApoB
LE (pH 5) – ligand-binding arm binds b-propeller and releases LDL
pH 7
pH 5

RME OF TRANSFERRIN/IRON
Ligand (transferrin) remains bound to receptor
Fe3+ released at low pH in LE

ACTICE QUESTION 12 KEY
You are investigating the uptake of LDL particles in a cell brought back from the latest Mars mission.  Preliminary studies show that the Martian cells contain cholesterol in their membranes just as Earth cells do.  Luckily, the Martian cells are able to incorporate cholesterol from Earth LDL particles into their membranes which makes experiments much easier.  You perform the following experiments.
A. You perform a pulse-chase experiment to define the pathway that ApoB follows after uptake of LDL particles by the Martian cells.  You find that the ApoB follows the same path as in Earth cells.  What are these localizations in order?
Clathrin-coated vesicles > uncoated vesicles > late endosome > lysosome
B. You perform in vitro binding studies with purified Earth LDL particles and Martian LDL receptors.  You find that the LDL particle binds to the receptor equally well at both pH 7 and pH 5.  Are these binding results the same or different from what you would expect with LDL particles and LDL receptors from Earth cells?  Explain.
These results are different than what occurs with Earth LDL particles and receptors.  Earth LDL particles do not bind to Earth LDL receptors at pH 5.
C. Propose a model for how receptor mediated endocytosis of LDL works in the Martian cells.
LDL particles bind to LDL receptors at the cell surface.  The receptors with particles bound are internalized in clathrin-coated vesicles.  The vesicles uncoat and fuse with the late endosome.  The lumen of the Martian late endosome is pH 3.  The low pH causes the LDL particle to be released from the receptor.  The particle is sent to the lysosome to be degraded and the receptor is returned to the cell surface.

PRACTICE QUESTION 10 KEY
You are using reconstitution experiments to study the formation of COPII-coated vesicles.  Specific components that you have available are shown in the table below.  The “ER membrane” is an artificial membrane that contains the Sec12 protein and V-SNAREs.  GTPgS is an analog of GTP that is functionally identical in every way except that it cannot be hydrolyzed.  You perform the following experiments.  After incubating the components indicated in experiment 1 you observe normal formation of COPII-coated vesicles that are able to fuse at a target membrane.
A Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 2.  Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Without Sar1 present, no steps of the process can occur.
B. Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 3.  Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Sec12 at the ER membrane activates Sar1 by causing Sar1 to replace GDP with GTP.  Sar1-GTP extends its hydrophobic tail and inserts into the ER membrane.  Sar1 binds to Sec23 of the Sec23/24 complex.  Sec23/24 curve the membrane and recruit Sec13/31 complexes.  Sec23/24 complexes form the inner coat of the vesicle and Sec13/31 form the outer coat.  The vesicle pinches off and is released into the cytosol.  Sar1 hydrolyzes its GTP, releases from the membrane, and the coat disassembles.  Without a Rab, the vesicle is not targeted to a membrane and will not fuse at any target.
C. Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular
transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 4.
Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Sec12 at the ER membrane activates Sar1 by causing Sar1 to replace
GDP with GTPgS.  Sar1-GTPgS extends its hydrophobic tail and inserts
into the ER membrane.  Sar1 binds to Sec23 of the Sec23/24 complex.
Sec23/24 curve the membrane and recruit Sec13/31 complexes.
Sec23/24 complexes form the inner coat of the vesicle and Sec13/31
form the outer coat.  The vesicle pinches off and is released into the
cytosol.  Because GTPgS cannot be hydrolyzed by Sar1 the coat is not
released.  With the coat present, Rab and V-SNARE are not able to
target and fuse the vesicle to the target membrane.

PRACTICE QUESTION 11 KEY
You have predicted the amino acid sequences of several proteins based on their open reading frames.  The significant domains are shown below.  Predict the final localization(s) and any orientation of domains for each. Assume that any soluble protein in the ER is transported to the Golgi.
Rectangle = 25 aa hydrophobic-rich sequence
+/- = regions of positively or negatively charged amino acids
N = amino terminus; C = carboxyl terminus
Others = single letter amino acid code
